title: SSC CGL T-I English Section Test

questions.question: Select the word closest in meaning to the word given below. Bowdlerize
questions.explanation: • Given word – Bowdlerize: • Bowdlerize means to remove or change parts of a text (especially sexual, rude, or offensive parts) so that it becomes “acceptable” for general readers. • (Hindi meaning: आपत्तिजनक अंश हटाकर/बदलकर शुद्ध करना; सेंसर करना) • Example: The publisher bowdlerized the novel to make it suitable for school students. • Correct option – Censor: • Censor means to examine and remove obwectionable or sensitive material from books, films, speeches, etc. • (Hindi meaning: सेंसर करना / आपत्तिजनक भाग हटाना) • This is closest to “bowdlerize” because both involve removing offensive/undesirable content. • Example: The film was censored before its release. • Synonyms of Bowdlerize: censor, sanitize, expurgate, edit. • Antonyms of Bowdlerize: publish uncut, preserve, retain, include. • Meanings of other options (why they are incorrect): • (a) Adulterate – to make something impure by adding inferior/harmful substances (Hindi: मिलावटी/दूषित करना) • (c) Crumble – to break into small pieces; disintegrate (Hindi: टूटकर बिखर जाना) • (d) Abuse – to misuse or treat badly; insult (Hindi: दुरुपयोग करना / गाली देना) So the correct answer is (b).

questions.question: Read the sentence and choose the option that correctly uses the homonym of the highlighted word. “At sunrise, a sharp wind swept across the riverside path as the pilgrims began their quiet walk.”
options: The wind grew colder as the sky turned grey.
Please wind the string tightly around the parcel so it doesn’t come loose.
They waited for the wind to calm before lighting the candle.
A sudden wind scattered the dry leaves across the steps.
questions.explanation: The correct sentence using the homonym of “wind” (विंड/हवा) is (b) wind /waɪnd/ = “to wrap/coil” (लपेटना/घुमाना), which is different in meaning and pronunciation from wind /wɪnd/ = “moving air” (हवा). Explanation: In (b), wind is a verb meaning “to coil/wrap,” correctly showing the homonym pair (same spelling, different meaning/pronunciation). Options (a), (c), and (d) use wind in the same sense as the original sentence (air/breeze), so they are not the homonym use. Meanings of other options: · (a) “wind” (हवा) — same sense as the original. · (c) “wind” (हवा) — same sense as the original. · (d) “wind” (हवा) — same sense as the original.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate antonym of the given word. SCORCHING
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b) Icy. SCORCHING means extremely hot or burning in effect; it is used for intense heat (for example, scorching sun) and can also describe severe criticism. (Hindi meaning: झुलसाने वाला / बहुत गर्म) Example: The trekkers struggled under the scorching afternoon sun. Icy means extremely cold like ice, or lacking warmth in manner. It is the direct opposite of scorching heat. (Hindi meaning: बर्फ़ीला / बहुत ठंडा) Example: An icy wind swept through the valley at night. Synonyms of SCORCHING: burning, searing, blistering, blazing. Antonyms of SCORCHING: icy, cold, cool, chilly. Meanings of other given options: • Option (a) Searing: extremely hot; burning; causing intense heat (Hindi meaning: झुलसाने वाला). • Option (c) Blazing: very hot; shining brightly; flaming (Hindi meaning: दहकता / प्रचंड). • Option (d) Incendiary: designed to cause fire; also inflammatory in speech (Hindi meaning: आग लगाने वाला / भड़काऊ). So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Choose the correct meaning of idiom given below: “Get the drop on someone”
options: Gain an advantage unexpectedly or secretly
questions.explanation: The correct option is (c). Get the drop on someone means to gain an advantage over someone by acting first, often by surprise or by having secret information. It commonly implies catching the person off guard. (Hindi idiom meaning: किसी पर अचानक बढ़त हासिल करना / पहले ही पकड़ में ले लेना) Example: The team studied the opponent’s tactics and got the drop on them in the first half. Why other options are incorrect: • Option (a): This means imitate, which is different from gaining surprise advantage. • Option (b): Trickery is not necessary; the idiom mainly indicates advantage by being first or surprising. • Option (d): It does not mean revealing plans; it is about secrecy or surprise. Other related idioms and meanings: • Catch someone off guard: to surprise someone when they are unprepared. • Have the upper hand: to be in a stronger position or advantage. • Beat someone to the punch: to do something before someone else can. So the correct answer is (c)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. Shayla sat ________ Komal during the performance, and they talked inaudibly throughout.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a) beside. The sentence indicates Shayla was sitting next to Komal. The preposition that expresses “next to” or “by the side of” is beside. Beside means at the side of; next to. (Hindi meaning: के बगल में / पास) Therefore, “sat beside Komal” is grammatically correct and contextually appropriate. Example: Please sit beside me so we can discuss quietly. Why other options are incorrect: • around – surrounding or in various places near. (Hindi: आसपास) It does not mean “next to one person” specifically. • between – in the space separating two things/people. (Hindi: के बीच) It typically requires two endpoints (between X and Y). • among – in the middle of many people/things. (Hindi: के बीच (बहुतों में)) Not suitable for sitting next to a single person. “So the correct answer is (a)”

questions.question: Select the most appropriate synonym of the given word. UNREMITTING
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b) Persistent. UNREMITTING means continuing without stopping, weakening, or becoming less intense; it suggests something constant and unceasing. (Hindi meaning: अविराम / निरंतर) Example: The region faced unremitting heat for several weeks. Persistent means continuing firmly or obstinately without interruption; lasting for a long time. It matches the idea of something that does not stop. (Hindi meaning: लगातार / निरंतर) Example: Her persistent efforts finally led to success. Synonyms of UNREMITTING: constant, continual, incessant, relentless. Antonyms of UNREMITTING: intermittent, occasional, sporadic, periodic. Meanings of all the other given options: • Option (a) Periodic: happening at regular intervals (Hindi meaning: आवधिक). • Option (c) Sporadic: occurring irregularly; not continuous (Hindi meaning: छिटपुट / अनियमित). • Option (d) Occasional: happening sometimes; not frequent (Hindi meaning: कभी-कभार). So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error. If there is no error select option d as your answer No sooner the metro doors closed (A)/than Aarav remembered his wallet was on the café counter, (B)/so he got off at the next station and called the barista to hold it. (C)/No error (D)
questions.explanation: Option (A) contains an error. Explanation: With negative/limiting adverbials like No sooner, we require inversion in the first clause: had + subwect + V3. Correct form: “ No sooner had the metro doors closed than …,” not “No sooner the metro doors closed …”. Parts (B) and (C) are fine: than properly introduces the result, and the simple past narrative sequence is natural. Grammatical rule used: • No sooner … than … → had + subwect + V3 in the first clause + than + simple past in the next clause. • Parallel patterns: Hardly/Scarcely … when … with inversion in the first clause. Example: “ No sooner had the lift started than it halted due to a power cut.” Booster: Do not pair No sooner with when (incorrect). Keep the auxiliary had before the subwect in the inverted clause.

questions.question: Fill in the blank (1) with correct form of verb:
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘has become’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘has become’ (present perfect) shows a change that has occurred over time and is relevant to the present situation. • As per the context, satellite traffic is described as reaching a congested state due to ongoing growth; hence ‘has become’ is best. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • ‘becomes’ – simple present; sounds like a habitual truth, not a developed change due to recent expansion. • ‘became’ – simple past; suggests a finished past change without present continuation. • Thus, all the other given forms do not match the intended time reference and sense of development.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb form for the subwect. (2)
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘have lingered’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘have lingered’ (present perfect) fits with “for decades,” showing an action/state that started in the past and continues up to the present. • Subwect agreement: “defunct satellites and spent rocket stages” is a plural subwect, so we use ‘have,’ not ‘has.’ Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • ‘linger’ – simple present; weaker with “for decades” because it does not clearly show duration up to the present. • ‘lingers’ – singular present; mismatches the plural subwect. • ‘lingered’ – simple past; suggests the lingering is finished, which contradicts ongoing debris presence. • Thus, only ‘have lingered’ matches both duration and agreement.

questions.question: What does ‘forgo’ most nearly mean here? (3) “…may be forced to forgo certain orbits altogether.”
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘give up or do without’ to make the sentence contextually correct. • ‘forgo’ = to give up; to choose not to have/do something (हिंदी: “त्याग देना/छोड़ देना”). • As per the context, if collisions rise, launches may have to avoid/abandon some orbits; hence “forgo” means “give up.” Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • (b) Celebrate publicly – unrelated to avoiding orbits. • (c) Repair and restore – opposite action; the context is avoidance, not fixing. • (d) Increase rapidly – unrelated meaning. • Thus, only (a) fits the context of avoiding certain orbits.

questions.question: Which auxiliary/modal fits best? (4)
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘must adopt’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘must’ expresses necessity/strong obligation (हिंदी: “अवश्य/जरूर करना”). The sentence is recommending urgent action to prevent a crisis. • As per the context, international coordination is presented as essential; therefore the modal ‘must’ is the strongest and most suitable choice. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • ‘adopts’ – simple present; sounds like a routine habit, not urgent necessity. • ‘adopting’ – participle; would need a different structure (e.g., “by adopting…”). • ‘has adopted’ – present perfect; wrongly suggests the action is already completed. • Thus, ‘must adopt’ best matches the urgency and intended meaning.

questions.question: . The word "relic" most nearly refers to: (5)
options: A small surviving remnant from the past
questions.explanation: The correct meaning is ‘A small surviving remnant from the past’. • Relic = something that remains from an earlier time; a leftover trace (हिंदी: “अवशेष/पुरानी चीज़ का बचा हुआ हिस्सा”). • As per the context, the passage warns that the once-open frontier may turn into a leftover reminder of human short-sightedness—hence “relic” fits as a “remnant/trace.” Why the others are incorrect: • (b) A brand-new invention — opposite of “relic,” which is old/leftover. • (c) A strict legal punishment — unrelated to the meaning of “relic.” • (d) A loud public celebration — unrelated and contextually incorrect. Synonyms: remnant, vestige, leftover, trace. Antonyms: innovation, novelty, creation.

questions.question: Rearrange the following sentences in correct order to make a meaningful passage. 1. The group forwarded the compiled results to the research unit. 2. Raw feedback forms were collected from various channels. 3. Each file was cleaned and standardized before analysis. 4. Duplicate entries were removed using automated checks.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). The correct sequence is 2-3-4-1 because it follows the standard data-processing workflow: collect data, clean/standardize it, remove duplicates through checks, and then forward the final compiled results. • Sentence 2: Collection is the natural starting point: raw feedback forms are gathered first. • Sentence 3: Cleaning and standardization are done before proper analysis and further processing, as stated explicitly (“before analysis”). • Sentence 4: After standardization, automated checks can reliably detect duplicates and remove them. • Sentence 1: Forwarding compiled results is the final step after preparation and validation. Sequence clue: Words like “raw,” “before analysis,” and “compiled results” indicate the correct chronological order. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: A sentence is provided in direct speech. From the four given options, choose the one that most accurately conveys the sentence in its corresponding indirect speech. “How surprising that no one noticed the missing document until today!” cried the assistant.
options: The assistant cried it was surprising that no one noticed the missing document until today.
The assistant cried that it was surprising that no one had noticed the missing document until that day.
The assistant said that it was surprising no one had noticed the document till now.
The assistant cried how surprising no one had noticed the document.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b). The given direct speech is an exclamatory sentence expressing surprise. While converting direct to indirect speech for exclamations: • We remove quotation marks and the exclamation mark. • We use a reporting verb suitable for emotion, such as exclaimed with surprise / cried / exclaimed. • We change the sentence into a statement using “that.” • We apply backshift of tense when the reporting verb is in the past (cried). • We change time expressions: today becomes that day. Direct: “How surprising that no one noticed the missing document until today!” Indirect should therefore be: The assistant cried that it was surprising that no one had noticed the missing document until that day. Option (b) correctly applies both tense backshift (noticed → had noticed) and time change (today → that day). Why other options are incorrect: • Option (a): Does not change “today” to “that day” and does not backshift to “had noticed.” • Option (c): Changes reporting verb and uses “till now,” which is incorrect for past reporting; also alters the time reference. • Option (d): Incomplete/incorrect structure; it does not properly form an indirect statement with “that it was surprising...” Booster tip: With past reporting verbs, time words change: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, yesterday → the previous day. So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Change the following from active to passive: Policy experts have been questioning the new proposal for its vague provisions and weak enforcement.
options: The new proposal has been being questioned for its vague provisions and weak enforcement by policy experts.
The new proposal was questioned by experts for vagueness.
The proposal has been questioned by experts for its weak enforcement.
The proposal is being questioned by experts for its vague provisions.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). The active sentence is: “Policy experts have been questioning the new proposal ...” The tense here is Present Perfect Continuous (have/has been + V1-ing). To convert active to passive, we move the obwect “the new proposal” to the subwect position, keep the tense equivalent, and add “by + agent” when needed. Structure: • Active voice: Subwect + have/has been + V1-ing + Obwect • Passive voice: Obwect + have/has been being + V3 + by + Subwect Therefore, the correct passive form is: “The new proposal has been being questioned ... by policy experts.” Option (a) matches this required structure and retains the complete meaning, including the continuous sense of an ongoing activity. Why other options are incorrect: • Option (b): Changes tense to Simple Past and also shortens meaning. • Option (c): Changes the meaning by dropping “vague provisions” and does not keep the continuous aspect. • Option (d): Uses Present Continuous Passive (“is being questioned”), not Present Perfect Continuous Passive. Booster note: Although “has been being” can sound heavy in modern usage, it is the grammatically correct passive equivalent tested in competitive exams. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Select the correct option to fill in the blank. “After the invigilator discovered the mismatch between roll numbers and answer-book barcodes, the candidates were made ___________________ in a silent, serpentine queue outside the corridor—only to be summoned one at a time for verification.”
questions.explanation: The correct option to fill in the blank is (d) to wait. Explanation: With causative ‘make’ in the passive ( were made), English requires a to-infinitive: were made to wait. In the active, we use the bare infinitive (“They made the candidates wait”), but passivisation inserts to. The long post-modifiers (“in a silent, serpentine queue … only to be summoned…”) do not affect the verb form right after were made. (Hindi: passive causative संरचना में made के बाद to + V1 आता है— were made to wait.) Example: “I was made to rewrite the entire section before submission.” Why the other options are incorrect: · (a) wait — bare infinitive suits the active causative (“made the candidates wait”), not the passive used here. · (c) waiting — participial/gerund form; does not fit the required to-infinitive after were made. · (b) to be waited upon — idiomatic elsewhere (receive service), but semantically wrong here and changes the meaning entirely. Information booster / exceptions: · Active: make/see/hear/let + obwect + V1 → “They made him apologize.” · Passive: obwect → subwect + be + V3 + to + V1 → “He was made to apologize.” · With help, both help me do/ help me to do occur in active; in passive, we prefer to-infinitive: “I was helped to do it.”

questions.question: Rearrange the following sentences to form a meaningful and grammatically correct paragraph: P. He prepared his body for the intense workout by following a specific diet and drinking plenty of water. Q. On weekends, he challenged himself by going for long-distance runs. R. During the week, he built his stamina by running shorter distances regularly. S. Exhausted yet thrilled, he crossed the finish line on race day.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b). The sentences describe a logical training sequence that leads to race day. First, the person prepares his body with diet and hydration (P). Then he builds stamina during the week with shorter runs (R). After that, he challenges himself on weekends with long-distance runs (Q), which naturally follows regular weekday training. Finally, the story concludes with the outcome on race day when he crosses the finish line (S). Correct sequence: P → R → Q → S Sentence-wise explanation: • P sets the preparation stage (diet and water) before training intensifies. • R shows routine weekday practice, building stamina step-by-step. • Q continues the training pattern by adding weekend long runs as a bigger challenge. • S provides the final conclusion with “race day” and finishing the race. How to identify the correct order: Look for time markers like “During the week,” “On weekends,” and “on race day,” which clearly indicate a chronological sequence. Preparation must come before regular training, and race day must come at the end. So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: A sentence is provided in indirect speech. From the four given options, choose the one that most accurately conveys the sentence in its corresponding direct speech. She asked whether, considering the delays caused by suppliers, the delivery deadline could be extended.
options: “Can the delivery deadline be extended because of the delays caused by suppliers?” she asked.
“Given the supplier delays, can you review the delivery schedule?” she asked.
“Would it be possible to extend the deadline due to supplier delays?” she asked.
“Is there any chance the delivery deadline can be extended due to the supplier delays?” she asked.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). The given sentence is in indirect speech with the reporting verb “asked” and the conwunction “whether,” which indicates a yes/no question in direct speech. While converting indirect to direct speech for yes/no questions: • We remove “whether/if.” • We use an interrogative form with an auxiliary verb (can/could/will/would, etc.). • We place the question mark at the end. Here, the core meaning is: she asked about the possibility of extending the delivery deadline due to supplier delays. Option (a) forms a clear direct question and keeps the intended meaning without adding extra words or changing the request type. Why other options are incorrect: • Option (b): Changes the meaning to reviewing the schedule rather than extending the deadline. • Option (c): Uses a different structure (“Would it be possible...”), which is close, but the given reported form “asked whether ... could be extended” is more directly matched by a “Can ... be extended?” conversion. • Option (d): Adds extra wording (“Is there any chance”) and slightly alters tone, making it less accurate as a direct equivalent. Important rule note: In exam-style conversions, the closest direct form usually keeps a simple auxiliary-based question that mirrors “asked whether.” So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Which of the following is incorrectly spelled?
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b). Option (b) is incorrectly spelled. The correct spelling is Quiescence, meaning a state of inactivity, quietness, or dormancy. (Hindi meaning: निष्क्रियता / शांत अवस्था) Example: After the festival, the town returned to quiescence. Meanings of all the given options: • Option (a) Chauvinism: excessive or prewudiced loyalty to one’s own group; extreme nationalism (Hindi meaning: कट्टर पक्षपात / अति-राष्ट्रवाद). • Option (b) Quiscence: incorrect spelling; correct word is Quiescence (Hindi meaning: निष्क्रियता). • Option (c) Intermittent: occurring at intervals; not continuous (Hindi meaning: रुक-रुक कर होने वाला). • Option (d) Ubiquitous: present everywhere; widespread (Hindi meaning: सर्वव्यापी). So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate one word for the given expression. A person who writes beautifully in a special style
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (b) Calligrapher Explanation: The expression “a person who writes beautifully in a special style” refers to someone skilled in decorative or artistic handwriting. • A calligrapher is a person who practices calligraphy, that is, the art of beautiful handwriting or lettering. Hence, it best matches the given expression. Meaning of the correct word (English + Hindi): • Calligrapher = a person who writes in beautiful artistic handwriting = सुलेखक, सुंदर लेखन करने वाला व्यक्ति Meanings of other options (English + Hindi) and why they are incorrect: (a) Biographer = a person who writes someone’s life story = जीवनीकार • Writes biographies, not decorative/beautiful handwriting. (c) Cartographer = a person who draws maps = मानचित्रकार • Related to maps and geography, not handwriting. (d) Choreographer = a person who designs dance steps = नृत्य-निर्देशक • Related to dance composition, not writing.

questions.question: Four words are given, out of which only one word is spelt correctly. Choose the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelt word is (a) Belvedere. • Correct word – Belvedere: Belvedere refers to a building, structure, or architectural feature designed to provide a pleasant view, often situated on higher ground. (Hindi meaning: सुंदर दृश्य देखने के लिए बना भवन या स्थान) Example: The palace had a belvedere overlooking the valley.

questions.question: Choose the most suitable option to replace the highlighted part of the sentence: Forty lakhs are the amount he needs to pay as the first instalment of this full-time course.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option (a). Step-by-step reasoning: • The subwect here is “Forty lakhs”, which refers to an amount of money. • When a plural-looking number refers to a single amount, measurement, or sum, it is treated as a singular unit. • Therefore, the verb should be singular: is, not are. Correct sentence: Forty lakhs is the amount he needs to pay as the first instalment of this full-time course. Grammatical rule used: • Numbers/amounts used as a single unit take a singular verb. • Examples: “Ten years is a long time.” “Five kilometers is not far.” “Two thousand rupees is enough.” Why other options are incorrect: • Option (b) were: Past tense is not needed because the sentence is about a present requirement. • Option (c) have: “Have the amount” is grammatically incorrect and does not fit subwect-verb agreement. • Option (d) has: Although “has” is singular, the phrase “has the amount” is incorrect in meaning and structure; we need a linking verb “is.” Information booster: In exams, always check whether a plural form is being used as a single quantity. If yes, use a singular verb. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Choose the correct passive voice transformation of the sentence: He reported spotting an elusive species of bird during his expedition to the Amazon rainforest.
options: It was reported by him that he had spotted an elusive species of bird during his expedition with the Amazon rainforest.
An elusive species of bird was reported to have been spotted by him during his expedition to the Amazon rainforest.
An elusive species of bird was spotted during his expedition to the Amazon rainforest, it was reported by him.
He reported that an elusive species of bird was spotted by him during his expedition to the Amazon rainforest.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b). The sentence contains a reporting verb (“reported”) followed by an obwect-like idea (“spotting an elusive species of bird”). In passive voice, such structures are most naturally converted using the pattern: Structure: • Active: Subwect + reporting verb + V-ing/obwect idea • Passive (preferred): Obwect/idea + was/were reported + to have been + past participle + (by + agent) Why option (b) is correct: “An elusive species of bird” becomes the subwect, and the reporting action is shown as “was reported.” Since the spotting happened before the reporting, we use the perfect infinitive passive: to have been spotted. The agent “by him” is correctly placed. Correct passive sentence: “An elusive species of bird was reported to have been spotted by him during his expedition to the Amazon rainforest.” Why other options are incorrect: • A: Uses an awkward and incorrect phrase “expedition with the Amazon rainforest” (should be “to/in”). Also, this option unnecessarily repeats “he” and is not the best passive transformation. • C: Creates a comma splice/run-on and unnatural structure; exam-standard passive should be a single clear sentence. • D: Still keeps “He reported” active; it is not a proper passive transformation of the main reporting verb. So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Choose the correctly spelt word.
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (b) Irascible Explanation: Irascible is the correct spelling. It means easily angered or quick-tempered. The incorrect options either remove the letter c, add extra letters, or double the wrong consonant, which makes them incorrect. Meaning of the correct word (English + Hindi): Irascible = easily angered; short-tempered = चिड़चिड़ा, जल्दी गुस्सा होने वाला Common confusion to remember: The correct spelling contains scible, not sible or ssci, and it begins with iras, not irras. Bottom of Form

questions.question: The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error. If there is no error select option d as your answer Mira has decided (A)/ installing smart blinds in her rented apartment, (B)/not only to keep out the afternoon heat but also to reduce glare while she teaches online. (C)/No error (D)
questions.explanation: Option (B) contains an error. Explanation: After verbs like decide, plan, hope, agree, we use the to-infinitive (to + V1), not a gerund. Therefore, “ to installing” is incorrect; it must be “ to install.” The parallel infinitives in part (C)—“to keep out … but also to reduce …”—are correct and show purpose. Grammatical rule used: Verbs of decision/intention + to-infinitive (e.g., decided to install, planned to travel). Use a gerund after prepositions or certain verbs ( insist on installing, consider installing). Example: “She decided to install blackout curtains to sleep better during the day.” Booster: In parallel purpose expressions with not only … but also …, keep forms consistent: either repeat to (“to keep … to reduce …”) or omit it only when ambiguity is impossible.
